data.title: Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
We are monitoring the abilities of your portfolio companies to manage cost inflation and maintain profit margins consistent with our expectations.

This letter will highlight the results of the Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund for the twelve-month period ended 31 December 2025. It is published amidst the ongoing developments associated with the Russian war with Ukraine, an evolving inflation outlook in the U.S., Mideast tensions, and government policy uncertainty in the U.S. and elsewhere. The S&P 500 Index, the MSCI All Country World Index and the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index all reported positive total returns for calendar 2025, including Q4’2025. In brief, equities repriced higher in the face of uncertainty about the impact of U.S. trade policies. The U.S. dollar was broadly stable, and most bond markets rallied.

Your fund paid an ordinary income dividend of $0.61 per I share in December 2025 and a $2.45 long term capital gains dividend in Q4’2025, resulting in $3.06 in total dividends for calendar 2025. The net asset value of the I shares decreased $0.17 per share ($47.92 to $47.75) in Q4’2025 due to the dividends paid during the quarter. For the trailing 12-month period ending 31 December 2025 the net asset value of the I shares increased from $36.17 per share to $47.75. The ordinary income dividends per share were lower for A and C shares, to account for varying class specific expenses.

Your fund’s I share return of +6.49% for Q4 2025 exceeded the +3.29% return of the MSCI All Country World Index. For the trailing 12-month period ending 31 December 2025 your fund’s I share return of +41.08% exceeded the +22.34% return of the MSCI All Country World Index. Generally speaking, multi-year returns of Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund have exceeded the returns of the MSCI All Country World Index.

We are now in the 20th year of managing Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund. From its inception on 28 July 2006 through 31 December 2025, Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund has outpaced the MSCI All Country World Index by an average margin of more than 3% per year, resulting in a total cumulative return since inception of 693% (I shares) versus 351% for the MSCI All Country World Index.

Performance comparisons of Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund to its benchmark over various periods are shown elsewhere on this site.

As of 31 December 2025 the weighted harmonic average price/earnings ratio for the equity investments of Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund was 19.9x. This compares to a weighted harmonic average multiple of 21.6x for the MSCI All Country World Index using 2025 expected EPS. We believe that our strategy of owning a focused portfolio of firms with above average revenue growth that are value priced with respect to revenue/earnings/cash flow vis-à-vis the overall market has been a key ingredient to long term outperformance by Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund.

Listed in Table 1 in descending order are the 25 largest equity holdings in the fund as of 31 December 2025, along with their returns in calendar years 2025 and 2024. Returns are shown in $US, assuming reinvestment of dividends in shares of the firm. We also show the trailing 1-year and 5-year average annual revenue growth rates for these businesses through the latest reported period. For rough comparison:

  • The trailing 5-year weighted average revenue per share growth rate of the 2,517 firms included in the MSCI All Country World Index was +4.7%/year in $US terms through the end of the most recent reported trailing 12 month period. The average 1-year revenue per share growth rate of these same firms was +2.2%.
  • The trailing 5-year weighted average annual revenue per share growth rate of the 40 equity holdings in the Thornburg Global Opportunities portfolio was +11.6%/ year through the most recent reported trailing period, which ended 30 September 2025 for a majority of the firms listed. The weighted average 1 year revenue per share growth rate of these firms was +9.5% through the most recently reported 12-month period.
  • The weighted average portfolio revenue growth rates per share of your fund’s portfolio has significantly exceeded that of the MSCI All Country World Index.

Together, these firms comprised approximately 79% of the fund’s total assets as of 31 December 2025. Near- cash debt investments comprised 2% of fund assets, and 15 other equities comprised a total of approximately 19% of fund assets. Individual position sizes of the fund’s top 25 positions range from approximately 5.5% (Alphabet) to approximately 1.7% for those shown near the bottom of this list.

These are not trivial businesses. Weighted average revenue growth for the Thornburg Global Opportunities portfolio exceeded global GDP growth for the most recently reported trailing 1 year and 5-year periods. As previously described, the average revenue/share growth rates of these businesses significantly exceeded the revenue growth of the MSCI All Country World Index over the trailing 1 and 5-year periods. Most of these businesses emerged from the economic valley of the Covid period with their competitive positions intact or improved and ready to address the challenges and opportunities that lie ahead. A few of these are value priced “fixer-uppers” where we monitor the progress of structural improvement.

The reader will notice a high incidence of investments in firms tied to the digital economy, in producers of critical resources, and in financial intermediaries that we believe should benefit from interest rates determined primarily by free market forces. For now, there appears to be periodic fluctuation of investor confidence in the outlook for industrial commodity sector businesses.

Twenty-one equities made positive contributions to Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund portfolio performance of at least 0.10% during Q4’2025. Leading contributors to portfolio performance for the quarter included technology firms Samsung Electronics (SSNLF), Alphabet (GOOG), CACI International (CACI), and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM); communications network operators Zegona Communications (ZEGLF), Orange (ORANY), and Converge Information & Communications; financials Citigroup (C), Bank of Ireland (BKRIF), BNP Paribas (BNPQF), NN Group (NNGPF), Capital One (COF), and Charles Schwab (SCHW); miners Freeport McMoran (FCX) and Mineral Resources (MALRF); consumer businesses Coca-Cola Icecek and TJX Companies (TJX); India conglomerate Reliance Industries; energy producer Total Energies (TTE); and pharmaceutical firm Eli Lilly (LLY).

Ten equities subtracted at least -0.10% from Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund portfolio performance during Q4’2025. The most significant detractors were e-commerce firms Alibaba Group (BABA), Tencent Holdings (TCEHY) and Meta Platforms (META); AT&T (T), consumer discretionary businesses Round One Corporation (RNDOF), Nien Made Enterprises and Galaxy Entertainment (GXYEF), industrial firms CATL and Techtronic Industries (TTNDY); and enterprise software developer SAP (SAP). We made various position size adjustments in recent months for portfolio diversification purposes, tax management, and to better balance the downside risk vs upside capital appreciation potential of individual positions.

Table 2 summarizes major sector weightings within the Global Opportunities Fund equity portfolio as of 31 December 2025 and selected prior quarter ends. The year-to-year reduction in Consumer Discretionary sector investments mostly reflects trims to position sizes of TJX Companies, Round One Corp, Mercedes Benz Group (MBGAF), Barratt Redrow (BTDPF), and Booking Holdings (BKNG). Increased investments in businesses in the Health Care sector, Information Technology sector, and Industrials sector were mostly funded by these trims.

Incoming economic data from around the world indicate a resilient global economy in the face of evolving U.S. trade policies, so far resisting falling into a recession. There are clear indications that global trade flows have been altered by new U.S. trade policies. Winners and losers among artificial intelligence linked businesses and multi-national producers of tradeable goods will become obvious in time, but the current outlook for many global businesses is uncertain. Prices of traded financial assets will be volatile with day-to-day news changing perceptions of near-term economic performance, interest rate policies, and political decisions We are paying attention to the abilities of your portfolio companies to manage cost inflation and maintain revenue and profit margins consistent with our expectations.

Our Investment Framework

  • Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund seeks capital appreciation from a focused portfolio of global equity investments. We believe the structure of the fund—built on our core investment principles of flexibility, focus, and value—provides a durable framework for value-added investing.
  • We urge shareholders of the fund to maintain a long-term investment perspective rather than placing too much emphasis on return figures that are available daily, weekly, monthly, and quarterly. A clear example of the need to keep a longer-term
  • investment perspective is illustrated by comparing the trailing 66-month return of Thornburg Global Opportunities fund as of 31 December 2025 [+212%] with the return from the single Covid onset quarter ending 31 March 2020 [-24.8%] that preceded this 66-month period. Most businesses in your portfolio have managed well through varying economic environments across business cycles. A few have potential to be much better businesses after being “fixed up.” We continue to follow our core investment principles of flexibility, focus, and value, as we have since your fund’s inception back in 2006.
  • Thank you for being a shareholder of Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund. Remember that you can monitor the holdings of Thornburg Global Opportunities Fund and other information respecting your fund on our website, www.thornburg.com.

data.title: FSSL: Buy A Dollar For Seventy-Seven Cents With This New High-Yield CEF
data.description:
Note: this article was previously published for members of the Inside the Income Factory investing group.

As an income investor seeking a high-yielding income stream to add to my river of cash flowing into my investment accounts each month (or each week, in some cases), I look for new opportunities at sale prices. One of those opportunities to “buy a dollar for 77 cents” is available now in the form of a recently converted CEF (closed-end fund) that currently trades at a -23% discount to NAV and pays a forward dividend yield of 11.74%. That CEF is newly listed in 2025, FS Specialty Lending Fund (FSSL).

From the fund’s investor materials, here is a brief description of the fund history:

On September 29, 2023, FS Energy & Power Fund was renamed FS Specialty Lending Fund as a part of a plan to transition the Fund’s investment strategy from investing primarily in private U.S. energy and power companies to a diversified credit strategy investing in private and public credit in a broader set of industries, sectors and subsectors.

Following that, the fund explained the approach used for a direct listing to a new CEF from what was formerly a BDC (business development company). This excerpt is from the Q225 Quarterly update.

FSSL PREPARES FOR LISTING

In connection with FSSL’s liquidity planning, the Fund will be converted from a BDC to a closed-end fund registered under the Investment Company Act of 1940 through a reorganization into a newly formed closed-end fund.

The closed-end fund will be named “FS Specialty Lending Fund”, and we currently expect its common shares to begin trading on the NYSE under the ticker “FSSL” before the end of Q4 2025, subject to market conditions.

Shares of FSSL began trading on November 13, 2025, and declared a Q4 quarterly distribution of $0.42 to be paid to shareholders on December 15, 2025. That would turn out to be the final quarterly distribution, as the CEF began making monthly distributions starting in January.

The Board of Trustees of FS Specialty Lending Fund (the Fund) announced the monthly distribution for January 2026. The distribution of $0.1375 per share will be paid on January 30, 2026.

The ex-div date for the January distribution will be January 23, so you have until then to capture the January dividend if you do not already own shares. I like what I see in FSSL and rate this CEF a Buy because of the large discount to NAV, the high-yield distribution, which appears to be well supported, and its similarities to its big brother fund, FS Credit Opportunities Corp. (FSCO).

I liked FSCO when it first became available for public trading, partly due to similarities with its other brother, FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK), which is a BDC. Both CEFs and the BDC have parentage with Future Standard, a global asset manager with $86B in AUM (assets under management) and 30+ years’ experience. According to their website, they offer differentiated opportunities due to deep access to private credit markets.

Q425 Private Credit Scare

Around the beginning of October, a big plunge took place with anything in the market that was associated with “private credit.” The First Brands bankruptcy was the first big shoe to fall, followed by Jamie Dimon’s infamous “cockroaches comment.” The fear in the market associated with private credit led investors to redeem more than $7B from the biggest Wall Street funds in Q4 2025, including those offered by Ares, Blackstone, Blue Owl, Apollo, and others according to the Financial Times.

“I think there is a lot of fear in the air and time will tell if those fears are well founded,” said Philip Hasbrouck, the co-head of Cliffwater’s asset management business.

FSCO was particularly hard hit by this negative market sentiment and sent the price of that CEF back to a discount after it had completely closed the previously huge discount to trade at a slight premium.

But as you can see, the NAV was barely impacted and only declined very slightly over the past few months. This is a classic example of how you can take advantage of the large discounts that CEFs sometimes trade at to capture some capital appreciation when the market comes to its senses and starts driving the price higher again, like it is doing now with FSCO. Meanwhile, that fund continues to pay a consistent and in the case of FSCO, growing monthly dividend.

In the case of FSSL, this looks to me like another FSCO in the making, but still trading at a huge discount that is likely to close in the months ahead as investors start to realize that the strong income stream is likely to continue. However, FSSL has a very short history as a publicly traded CEF so caution is warranted before jumping in with a huge investment.

FSSL Key Facts and Portfolio Composition

As of the market close on January 15, 2026, the CEF had about 76 million shares outstanding trading at a market price of $14.06 with NAV estimated at $18.26, resulting in a discount of -23%. The distribution yield of 11.74% assumes the same monthly distribution declared for January of $0.1375 per share will also be paid the next 11 months to derive the annual market yield.

The fund portfolio currently holds $1.9B in AUM with 78 portfolio companies and 90% of the assets are senior secured debt. The assets that are floating rate comprise 84% of the total and the average duration is six months.

Although the original inception date of the “fund” (actually its predecessor BDC) was July 2011, FSSL only started trading as a CEF on 11/13/25. Total expenses are 5.7%, which may seem high but include interest expense and other costs of private credit and debt service.

The fund describes itself on the website with this phrase:

Core middle market credit strategy with an opportunistic edge

The fund portfolio (from Q425 Disclosure Presentation) is well diversified with top industry allocations to Consumer services, Commercial & professional services, and Healthcare equipment & services.

Top holdings (as of 9/30/25 from fund website) may not be familiar names but no single holding makes up more than 2.5% of the total, which also helps to reduce concentration risk.

There was some NAV decline in 2025 due to the ROC (return of capital) that was included in the “enhanced distribution” that the fund paid, which included funds in excess of net investment income earned as explained on the slide from the Q425 presentation.

.

This is a little bit of a red flag and could be one reason why the discount remains so wide. The percentage of ROC in the distribution is one thing to watch in 2026 when the fund issues its first full quarterly report as a publicly traded CEF at the end of Q1.

Risks: Is There a Bubble in Private Credit?

One risk that reared its head last fall was the fear of private credit growing too much and becoming a bubble that is about to pop. Because FSSL is a private credit lender, the fund is susceptible to market sentiment shifting again and leading to another selloff in funds like FSSL and FSCO. The investment team at Future Standard addressed this topic in a report issued at the end of November 2025. According to that report, the share of leveraged credit as a percentage of GDP has actually been declining over the past several years.

Furthermore, as the report goes on to explain, private credit has been growing for decades and is not showing any signs of poor health in the current environment.

A credit bubble forms when loan growth far outpaces true economic demand, inflating asset valuations, encouraging still more lending, and ultimately creating overcapacity and debt that incomes cannot support. As defaults rise—often amplified by hidden leverage—losses erode lender equity, confidence breaks down, and asset prices undergo a painful, prolonged correction.

We understand investors’ concerns about private credit’s rapid growth, but that growth has been decades in the making. As regulators have strengthened the banking system, banks have consolidated, retreated from certain business lines, and prioritized capital efficiency. Simultaneously, investors across the spectrum have sought to optimize income generation and diversification in their portfolios. Private credit sits at the intersection of these forces. Like all lending markets, it will experience periods of stress and defaults, but there is little to indicate that recent anecdotes reflect anything systemic.

Summary: FSSL Appears to be Another FSCO in the Making

While it is still very new to the public market and does not have much history as a CEF to examine yet, FSSL appears to offer a unique opportunity to acquire the fund at a substantial discount to NAV, which you can think of as buying a dollar for $0.77. That is exactly what happened with FSCO, which went from trading at a massive discount of greater than -30% to trading at a slight premium, before being pummeled by market sentiment during the Q4 private credit scare and now trades again at a discount.

There is somewhat of a speculative nature to FSSL because there is not a lot of detailed documentation on financials as a CEF yet. The Q4 2025 report should provide some additional information when those numbers are reported, but the fund was only publicly traded for half of the quarter (which does not equal an eighth in this case, sorry math nerds).

With a forward distribution yield of nearly 12% and a diversified portfolio of public and private credit investments, I like the prospects for FSSL going forward as the discount to NAV is likely to close over the next few months as more investors get comfortable with fund management.

data.title: Meta's AI CapEx Is Not A Growth Bet - It's A Defensive War For Relevance
data.description:
Thesis

In this article, we argue that Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) investments in AI are not visionary growth investments that the market currently prices them to be, but rather a reactive defensive move in response to disruptive change in the competitive landscape. We find overwhelming evidence for our claims, like OpenAI (OPENAI) entering the advertising market and findings from our reverse valuation. We initiate META with Strong Sell.

Structural change in the advertising market dynamics

Advertising is the core of Meta's business, accounting for 97% of total income.

And now, there seems to be a new, powerful rival showing up. Privately held OpenAI, powered by Microsoft Inc. (MSFT) investment, just announced it will begin testing ads within its ChatGPT, targeting free adult users in the United States. With an estimated number of users at 800 million and only 5% of those users paying, the decision is obvious in our view. If we put Meta's 3.54 billion daily active users with an average quarterly revenue by user of $14.46 and growing with a CAGR of 15.3% over the last 4 years, based on our calculations, it becomes an important issue of marginal growth. Meta already monetizes at full, while OpenAI is starting from zero within a disruptive technological and product-economic landscape of advertising that it almost solely created.

The AI is rewriting the economics of advertising by collapsing creative production, targeting, and campaign optimization into a single automated pipeline, shifting competition away from raw media. OpenAI did not just participate in this shift; it triggered it. By turning generative AI into a scalable, general-purpose product, it forced advertising economics to move away from media scale and toward automated execution. Meta’s move to allow brands to generate entire ad campaigns with AI directly inside Facebook and Instagram is one of the examples of that fundamental shift. The shift that is most evident in one of the big 6 advertising agencies, WPP plc (WPP), whose earnings are more than half in the post-AI world. But they are fighting for their survival by developing their own AI.

And all the big names are adjusting too. Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN) announced recently an Agentic AI tool for advertisers that executes the entire creative process in a fraction of the time it took without AI. With $17.6 billion of revenue in the quarter, growing 22% YoY, Amazon is taking part of the market in a fast move. Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) is structurally defending itself with Google Ads by expanding total query volume while keeping monetization per query broadly intact. Alphabet is using AI Overviews and AI Max to capture new commercial intent without relying on legacy keyword mechanics and without sacrificing advertiser ROI. This makes the Ads engine more adaptive and harder to disrupt precisely as user behavior shifts toward conversational and agentic search. All of these are signs of adaptations to structural change in advertising.

Global advertising spend is estimated to be at about $1.3 trillion in 2026. This would result in Meta having a global market share of approximately 14.5%, based on our calculations. Alphabet's advertising income is about 75% of total sales, by our calculations, so based on trailing sales of $385.5 billion, we can estimate about $290 billion in advertising or a market share of about 22%. It is estimated by WARC that Alphabet, Meta, and Amazon take about 56.1% of market share excluding China, with forecasts to rise to 58% this year. The cards in this space seem to be dealt, but AI is a major disruption.

One thing is certain in our view. Competition is fierce. Amazon, Alphabet, Microsoft, and Oracle Corporation (ORCL) are all investing heavily in the same market where the future payoff is uncertain. While doing that, they are creating bottlenecks on the supply side, so we can see that RAM prices are currently soaring. That creates higher costs, and higher costs get lower rates on return.

Here we would like to stress another point that may not be that obvious or is rarely mentioned. CapEx is measured in U.S. dollars, not capacity units. Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), as one of the top suppliers of the AI boom, currently has a net income margin of 53%. In 2022, NVDA had a net margin of 36%, and two years earlier, it was about 25.7%. So, the same capacity would cost you about 50% more in 2025 than in 2022 or double than in 2020-2021. We are aware that this is a bit of a simplistic view, but it is the best we can do with the given data. The point is, yes, new capacity will be coming online, but not as big as CapEx numbers would suggest, but at much higher prices than a couple of years ago.

When put all together, with a single line from the Q3 2025 earnings call transcript by Mr. Zuckerberg, Meta Founder, Chairman & CEO, “... a significantly larger investment here is very likely to be a profitable thing over some period...” tells us that these high investments are not visionary moves streamlined to increase growth or ROIC. In our view, “very likely to be profitable” is not a strong argument for investments that are streamlined to the company's growth while sharply increasing leverage, as we calculate in the latter part of the article. It is a defensive move in our view, a forced adaptation to a change in the competitive landscape, with only one purpose: to survive in the long run.

We believe our FCFE models in the next chapters of this article will prove the defensive nature of AI investments, but first, we have to deal with new lease commitments that have appeared on the last financial report on October 29th, 2025, after which the stock tumbled. We will calculate their full effect on net debt/EBITDA and annual cash flows based on information we have.

Leases

Effective January 1st, 2019, the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) issued a standard: ASC 842 Lease. Namely, the idea of this standard is to treat most of the lease companies' holdings as a debt on the balance sheet liabilities side and as a right-of-use asset on the assets side of the balance sheet. In essence, it treats rent as the buying of assets financed by debt. Before this standard, companies would generally pass as a light-asset model, boosting their ROA by showing their lease obligations off-balance. But that lease mostly has multi-year obligations that do not go away if the business finds itself in any kind of trouble.

Note 9 from Meta's Q3 2025 earnings report, titled “Commitments and Contingencies—Leases and Contractual Commitments,” highlighted a slight loophole in the standard. Since leases have not yet been commenced, they are still not in the balance sheet, although many of them are non-cancellable. Here, we would argue that GAAP ASC 842 is due to an update since this kind of treatment of liabilities is inconsistent with the idea of the standard but is fully in line with current guidelines in our view.

Based on Note 9, Meta has a total of $179.30 billion of leases that are not included in the balance sheet since they haven't yet commenced.

To acknowledge the lease as a liability and right-of-use asset, the nominal value of the lease obligation needs to be discounted by the applicable rate to present value over the estimated period of the lease.

To calculate the present value of the $179.30 billion lease, first, we need to estimate the period of the lease.

So, we use this estimation by the privately held McKinsey & Company on the structure of data center investments, since most of the leases are for data centers. That allows us to calculate the blended economic life of the assets and the blended depreciation rate based on estimates of current rates. Please note that the calculation of data in the next table is explained in detail below the table.

There seem to be slight rounding differences in the McKinsey report on the total, but we ignore them since we find them not to be relevant. Based on that report, construction works, PPE, and similar items are the smallest part of data center building costs at 15.15%. Next is power, with 19.70% of the cost of the total. And as one would expect, the largest part of data center cost is servers and storage, with 65.15% of the total data center cost.

The easiest part of the economic life of the asset calculation is for servers and storage, as it is stated in the report.

Note 6 of the Q3 2025 report clearly states that servers and network assets have useful lives of 5.5 years. While we are mentioning this, we would like to point out that at the start of the year 2025, there was an accounting change that prolonged the useful life of the servers and storage, which had a positive effect on the net income of $1.96 billion for the first nine months, or about 5% of 9m net earnings.

To calculate economic life for the rest of the data center assets, we annualize the difference of total depreciation of PPE of $4.76 billion and depreciation of servers of $3.6 billion, which gives us $4.64 billion ($1.16 times 4) on a 4-quarter basis for the rest of the data center. We divide this number by the current value of buildings ($50.75 billion) and get 9.1 years, so we decide to round it up to 10 years of useful economic life. Based on experience, we believe this is a fair assessment.

When we put those numbers in the calculation as presented above, we get roughly 7 years of blended economic life of the data center with an average annual depreciation rate of 15.33%.

Next step in calculating the present value of leases. As a discount rate, we use the interest rate on the seven-year Meta bond maturing in 2033 with an interest rate of 4.95%. The simple present value calculation gives us $127.87 billion. Currently stated net debt in the balance sheet is $7.76 billion, and if we add our calculation on top of it, we get net debt of $135.63 billion on the Meta balance sheet. With a 12-month EBITDA of $98.4 billion, we finally get an adjusted net debt/EBITDA of ~ x1.4. Note that it is not the current level, which we find problematic, since we believe that anything below 3 is acceptable in our view. What we find problematic is the speed of the trajectory, moving from the net debt/EBITDA of x0.08 as presented in the latest balance sheet data to x1.4. Here, we find it is important to note that over the last twelve-month period, the company has bought $44.23 billion worth of its shares and reduced its cash levels by $33.19 billion, so part of the debt increase is due to share repurchases.

The other important thing we calculate from the total lease obligation and useful life of the data center is the annual lease payment. Although we are aware that not all lease assets will be handed to Meta at once but over a longer period of time, for the sake of clarity and the current information we have, we calculate the annual lease payment of $25.62 billion over the average period of 7 years.

With that now in mind and with CapEx guidance from the last earnings call, we move to reverse valuation, in which we further prove that most of the AI investments are in defense of market position, not growth.

Reverse valuation

Meta shareholders' return and momentum over the one-year period were lackluster, so we would like to check what is currently priced in and what growth levels are needed to support CapEx and where value is destroyed.

We are using the FCFE model. Please note that we ignore all changes in working capital and debt levels. This is to focus on the core business, and due to our opinion that changes in working capital and debt levels shouldn't have a meaningful impact on valuation levels.

But first, we are going to set some of the perspective from the past FCFE levels.

As is shown by our calculations, the company has made sharp earnings growth over the 2025-2020 period (for 2025 we use market estimates) with a CAGR for that period of 17.1%. However, due to the strong CapEx that accounts for 73% of total D&A+CapEx+M&A, FCFE has grown only 5.5%, based on the middle of the guidance range for 2025 CapEx from the last earnings call and our estimates on D&A and market consensus for earnings.

At the moment of writing this article, the price of shares is around $620, RFR is 4.2% (US 10-year treasury (US10Y)), and the 5-year beta is 1.2.

Market consensus compiled by Seeking Alpha gives us EPS for 2025 of $25.58 and for FY 2026 of $30.38. Based on 2,522 million shares, we get earnings for FY 2025 at the level of $64,261 million and for FY 2026 at the level of $76,442 million. Please note that all stated lease payments are incurred in earnings as an expense.

We adjust D&A for FY 2026 to the upside to align with the full D&A ratio in D&A+M&A. From FY 2027, we expect D&A and CapEx to grow in line with earnings. To reach the stated price, company would need to grow its earnings at 31.9% in the period of 2027-2030. After that, it would need to grow over 10 years at 25.52%, going down to the level of the risk-free rate over every year. Our logic in using a perpetual growth rate equal to the RFR is to mitigate large changes in share price due to slight changes in the RFR.

So, math is simple. The current market price implies much faster earnings growth than the company has had in its history, with FCFE growth of 43.5% over the next five years vs. 10% over the last five years. This is something we see as highly unlikely for the company that has a high market share operating in a dramatically altered landscape with new, powerful competitors entering the market and with current and new competitors investing in the same AI tools as Meta.

Now, we are going to run one exercise to further demonstrate the defensive nature of AI investments.

So we did a reverse valuation that cuts CapEx in 2027 from $101.530 million to $30,000 million, the level we believe is enough to replenish D&A. We also did not adjust D&A for the next year. Please note that all stated lease payments are incurred in earnings as expenses. In that case, the company would need to grow its earnings at the level of 9.4% for the period 2027-2030 to justify the current price, a level that is much easier to achieve for a company like Meta, while FCFE would grow at a staggering CAGR of 57.4%. Also, this creates FCFE almost 60% larger in the terminal year than it does in the terminal year with CapEx at a 74/26 ratio, compared to 63/37.

So we believe that this proves that AI CapEx is not about visionary growth; it is playing defense at all costs. However, if this materializes, please note we see a sharp drawdown in the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX).

Also, as we stated earlier, the company was a large buyer of its shares over the last 12 months and in the years prior to that, which, in our view, sustained momentum on shares and made momentum followers and passive investments propel the share price up. More details on this can be read in our article named: When Value Becomes Momentum: Why Value ETFs No Longer Hedge Market Risk.

So, when we finally summarize all our findings in this article—a new powerful competitor on the market that is investing in the same strategies and technology that Meta and other old competitors invest in at growing prices with uncertain payoff, in a move we believe we have proved to be a defensive move rather than a visionary growth investment, and with levels that price in a sharp acceleration of growth, the risk-reward skews decisively toward sharp multiple compression rather than upside. We initiate with Strong Sell.

Risk to the bearish thesis confirms our bearish thesis

The most important risk to our bearish thesis is the one we have already mentioned in the previous part. If the company cuts CapEx severely to levels we find large enough to replenish D&A, much lower growth rates would be needed to support the current share price. However, that case also implies that there would be no meaningful loss of competitive position for the company in the long run.

The second risk is that there is a possibility that the company manages to pull an earnings CAGR of +35% and that those investments really do pay off at a much higher level than the market is currently pricing. We find this risk to be rather small since there is fierce competition, but we cannot totally ignore it.

Another risk to our bearish thesis is that the company manages to create an efficient way of using technology that no other competitor would be able to replicate, such as developing its own patent-protected technology.

Effect on the broad equity market

If anything, we believe this article made us realize that, in our view, a large part of AI investment is not a visionary offensive move but rather a defensive move with no certain payoff, where maintaining market share is the main priority. And that has large repercussions on the broader market. Meta is a significant weight in the Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS) and the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ), and so are other hyperscalers. Once investors internalize that AI CapEx is about survival rather than growth for hyperscalers, the narrative supporting premium multiples weakens, and valuation compression becomes the dominant risk, which is why we are Sell on both ETFs.

Also, even if you are completely contrarian to our view, we would advise you not to invest through Direxion Daily META Bull 2X ETF (METU).

The performance of the fund shows that when the stock price is flat and slightly negative, the fund underperforms massively. Since our view is strong sell on Meta stock, we find METU to be a Strong Sell also.

To wrap it up

Meta did not initiate the AI CapEx to maximize returns, but to avoid strategic irrelevance. AI compute has become a prerequisite for defending core businesses, not a discretionary growth option, so once one large player moved, others were forced to follow regardless of price, supplier margins, or near-term economics. In that environment, ROI discipline breaks down: management teams prioritize continuity of market position and narrative safety over measurable returns, accepting inflated CapEx and supplier pricing as the cost of staying in the game rather than risking being structurally left behind.

Our reverse valuation shows that the current share price already assumes a step-change in Meta’s earnings and FCFE growth that is materially above its historical delivery. It implicitly prices in rapid and efficient monetization of massive AI investments despite rising capital intensity, higher input costs, and an increasingly competitive advertising environment. In other words, the market is valuing outcomes that require near-perfect execution, leaving little margin for error and skewing risk to the downside.

We initiate Strong Sell for Meta Platforms and Strong Sell for METU.

data.title: Flex: Less Cheap, Still Delivering Ahead Of Q3 Earnings
data.description:
Flex Ltd. (FLEX), one of the largest global electronics manufacturing services (EMS) companies, has been an interesting business to check back in on. Since my very first article on the company, in January 2024, shortly after its Nextracker spin-off, the stock has delivered strong returns for investors. The rise in share price is connected to improving margins, EPS and free cash flow, rather than hype. Since 2019, under CEO Revathi Advaithi, Flex repositioned itself away from low-margin commodity electronics towards higher-value, more complex products and services, aligning with data centre and AI demand. The numbers since then speak for themselves.

Although the company's top line has remained relatively flat, its bottom line has increased from $93 million in FY2019 to $876 million trailing twelve months (TTM). In the same period, its levered free cash flow has increased from $165.9 million to $998.4 million TTM. The company has beaten EPS expectations for 20 consecutive quarters. Furthermore, while the stock is not as cheap as it was when I first wrote about it, it is still less expensive than similar peers in the industry. After reporting Q2 2026 results, Flex raised its FY 2026 guidance to reach revenue between $26.7 and $27.3 billion and adjusted EPS results for the year between $3.09 and $3.17. It will release its Q3 2026 earnings results on February 4. While I remain cautious given its exposure to cyclical industries, I maintain a bullish stance for long-term investors.

Company updates

Flex has shifted from a lower-margin electronics manufacturer to a higher-value products and services such as AI data centres, power infrastructure, automotive systems and healthcare. Flex generates revenue through two segments, namely Agility Solutions and Reliability Solutions, broken down within the image below. The products are power, cooling and integrated racks, which require more engineering-intensive work, but are much more profitable than the previous commodity assembly the company had in-place.

In December at Barclays, the management positioned the company with growth expectations for multiple years. According to research by Mordor Intelligence, the EMS market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 5.71% to reach $903.05 billion by 2031. Flex is considered one of the largest players within this market and has a global scope, tapping into the fastest-growing Asian market. Near term, we are also looking at confidence in improved results with the company raising its FY2026 Guidance.

Financials

The company's revenue dropped after the spin-off of Nextracker in FY 2024 and FY 2025, but this has been expected. For FY 2026, the company expects to deliver revenue between $26.7 and $27.3 billion, which indicates an improving growth trend. The company's shift to higher margin products is clear, with gross profit rising to $2.406 billion TTM, with a gross profit margin of 9.14%, which is up from 7.36% in FY 2021.

Looking at net income, we can see that FY 2024 is an outlier, owing to one-time income from the Nextracker spin-off. More importantly, we can see over the long term how the company's shift in operations has really improved its net income, which is at $876.0 million TTM, compared to $319.6 million in FY 2017.

Levered free cash flow has risen to $998.4 million TTM. Positively, we can see that there is an upward trend since FY2024. This allows the company to invest in AI and power-infrastructure investments in addition to supporting the company's shareholder reward initiatives.

The balance sheet shows a total cash of $2.25 billion. At the same time, the company's debt is high at $4.29 billion. However, its strong cash generation makes this manageable and allows the company to invest back into the business and also to continue reducing its debt.

Valuation

Flex's stock price has more than tripled since I first wrote about the stock in January 2024. As in my previous articles, I have compared the stock to other electronic manufacturing peers, namely Celestica Inc. (CLS), Jabil Inc. (JBL), and Sanmina Corporation (SANM). We can see that Flex trades at lower valuation levels than its peers, but across the board we can see that all stocks have seen significant highs over the last 52 weeks.

All the peers help build the complex systems behind cloud, data, and industrial technology, though each has a slightly different focus. We can see that although Flex's stock has increased in price over the year, it still has a lower FWD price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio at 25.26 compared to its peers. Celestica has the highest with a P/E of 50.91. We can see that the price-to-sales TTM is 0.96, indicating that you are paying less than a dollar for every dollar the company earned.

Risk

Flex has a number of risks that investors should be aware of. One of the key issues to be aware of is that the company is exposed to the automotive and industrial manufacturing industries, which are cyclical in nature and could negatively impact the company's overall financials in the long run if there is a long term downturn. Furthermore, as competition increases within the market, it might be difficult to maintain the high margins that the business currently benefits from. Another risk could be that the company is increasing its production capacity, this could be costly if the expected demand does not follow.

Final thoughts

When I first wrote about Flex just after its spin-off and again after its investment into AI, the stock was incredibly cheap relative to other peers in the industry. The upside has become less obvious, however, the company is still delivering strong bottom line results and generous cash flow, indicating that its business focus is paying off. It has also indicated strong results for FY 2026 and FY 2027 in recent management calls. The upcoming Q3 2026 results should give a better understanding of how the company will deliver. Nonetheless, the quarterly numbers have continued to impress and the stock is still cheaper than other peers in the industry. Therefore, I maintain a bullish stance on this stock.

data.title: Comparing NuScale Power Vs. NANO Nuclear Energy, I Will Stay Away From This Industry
data.description:
Introduction

The bigger the CapEx gets for hyperscalers, the more chips Nvidia (NVDA) sells, and Broadcom (AVGO) helps create. The more chips are coming out to the market, the more data centers are being built. The more data centers are built, the faster we are arriving at the next bottleneck for AI development - power. And it seems like the discussion of this fast-approaching shortage has loomed over the market for a couple of years now, but at the same time, energy has not been the market’s favorite commodity since 2022, according to Bloomberg.

But we are sure that the shortage will happen because there are many metrics that point in that direction. Data center electricity demand estimations vary from firm to firm, although they all agree on one thing: the demand is growing. Supply, at the same time, is not, mostly because of the inability to ramp up electricity production, but more importantly, delivery. It means it’s physically impossible to deliver much more power with the grid that we have today. Even if, let’s say, tomorrow we found a perfect solution for generating lots of electricity out of thin air, the grid would be a bottleneck that would take years to fix.

For this reason, many new, young companies focus either on renewable energy (which can be installed right near data centers to power them directly) or on other various solutions, including Small Modular Reactors. Today, I will be breaking down the latter for the first time. I am not a physicist, but I want to understand why this development is important, what exact needs it can cover, and how to find the best player in this industry. For this, I decided to compare NANO Nuclear Energy Inc. (NNE) with NuScale Power Corporation (SMR), as I was convinced they are direct competitors.

Jumping ahead, after the research, I realized that comparing them is like comparing a commercial airline with a private jet startup - possible, but it makes little sense. At the same time, I would not have understood this if I hadn’t looked at the tech characteristics of each product, and I think it’s important knowledge for each investor in the sector. So today, we will focus mostly on the developments, use cases, clients, and potential, and less on numbers.

Nuclear Power

Nuclear energy is one of the most promising solutions for powering data centers as of today. I’ve found various interesting studies on the matter, but I’d like to highlight the one by the International Atomic Energy Agency because it is focused on the reliability and resilience of the electrical grid when combined with nuclear plants.

I won’t quote the paper because it often gets too technical for our investment goals, but I’ll try to explain the main idea of it: We have low-inertia and high-inertia electricity sources. Low-inertia sources include most of the renewable energy sources, for example, solar panels, wind farms, etc. The rapid increase in the number of those reduces the predictability of generation and system inertia, which leads to changes in system frequencies. Nuclear power plants (NPPs), at the same time, can resist those changes for their high-inertia characteristics, and this stability will be more and more valuable going forward. At the same time, NPPs and the grid have partnership relations - the grid by itself must be stable; otherwise, it can lead to a LOOP (Loss of Off-Site Power) event, but it’s another story, not related to this analysis.

So after reading the previous paragraph, you won’t be surprised to learn that the nuclear power capacity is expected to grow rapidly over the next few years. There are various discussions that nuclear may become the main generator of power for data centers; for example, this Bloomberg chart below shows how many forecasted additions we will have in the next few years of nuclear capacity in the world in the next few years.

While it is impressive, it looks like the vast majority of that capacity is going to China - they are building 120 GW, and this is almost as much as the next 9 countries on the list build together. The U.S. is not even close to the beginning of the list, with only 8 GW of newly added capacity (the U.S. is the leader in absolute terms at the moment, with about 97 GW of operational capacity).

This is very important information for every investor, as when you see those charts and predictions for nuclear power capacity rapid growth in the next few years, you have to ask yourself if you will at all be able to participate in the rally if you don’t invest in Chinese or other countries' companies. The market may be sending American companies up as the shortage gets real, but it doesn’t look like the U.S. will be rapidly growing in this area.

At the same time, the positive characteristics of nuclear plants I’ve discussed before are mostly relevant for the standard big plants and not Small Modular Reactors (SMRs), and I wasn’t able to find any hard data to justify the predictions for growth in this specific segment of the market. Some predictions are very bullish, with a CAGR of 23%+. While it may indeed materialize, at the same time, considering the fact that most companies are years away from their first approved product, these growth estimations look too optimistic for my taste. But let’s now take a look at each of the companies I wanted to break down and compare them.

NuScale Power

NuScale is the first and only company to receive NCR approval of the design of its SMR, although no Small Modular Reactors have been built or are operational in the United States. While two NuScale designs are approved, the first planned commercial project was cancelled, and newer projects are still in the early construction or licensing phases, meaning they do not yet have permission to build those, even though the designs are approved:

The NRC completed a rigorous review of NuScale’s Design Certification Application (DCA), culminating in the issuance of the Final Safety Evaluation Report in August 2020 and the Standard Design Approval in September 2020. This approval confirmed the safety and reliability of NuScale’s 50-MW SMR design and marked a major milestone, both for the company and for advancing clean nuclear technology. The review process took 41 months, setting a record as the fastest design review in the NRC’s history.

In the meantime, another company, Holtec, has gotten the approval for the construction of its SMR-300 units, even before the design approval, because it has chosen a two-step licensing process that allows it to apply for a Construction Permit (CP) based on a preliminary design rather than a fully certified design. Whether this will be a winning strategy, we will have to wait to see, but the important part of this is that the bureaucracy is the main risk for those firms in the near term, at least in the U.S.

What is interesting about Nuscale’s product is that its technology is based on Light Water Reactors (LWRs). This is critical for investors to understand because LWRs are the standard technology used in the vast majority of existing large-scale nuclear plants today. It means that the only product that has ever been approved in this industry is not some new exotic miracle of physics. It’s a smaller version of something that was approved and operating for decades. It is not a bad thing for NuScale, but we will remember this fact when we discuss NANO Nuclear Energy.

NuScale creates power plants, which consist of power modules. Each module generates about 77 MWe. A standard plant might have 6 or 12 of these modules. Remembering the "inertia" concept from the IAEA report, NuScale’s modules are large enough to provide that stabilizing inertia to the grid, although it’s of course much weaker than a regular plant. Because they use Light Water technology, they use much more water than the advanced designs, so they would fit well where coal plants used to be.

Talking of their financial state, the most significant financial development for the quarter was the massive increase in liquidity. NuScale ended Q3 with $753.8 million in cash and equivalents, from $489.9 million the previous quarter. This was driven primarily by an aggressive share issuance in which the company sold 13.2 million Class A shares, generating $475.2 million. While dilution is usually not a good piece of news for existing shareholders, it’s the only way this company will be able to survive until 2030 until real revenue is expected to come.

This increase was driven by the sale of 13.2 million NuScale Class A shares through an at-the-market program during the third quarter, which generated $475.2 million in gross proceeds. Partially offsetting this increase was $128.5 million payment in relation to the PMA milestone triggered by the recent landmark agreement announced by TVA and ENTRA1.

Already now, NuScale reported revenue of $8.2 million (up over 1,600% year-over-year - ah, the growth of the new companies, right?), primarily driven by engineering services for the RoPower project in Romania. However, the company reported a significant EPS loss of -$1.85. A major factor in the company's cash outflow was a $128.5 million payment made to their partner, ENTRA1. This is part of a "Partnership Milestone Agreement" (PMA) designed to accelerate the 6 GW deployment with the Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA).

The company also announced an additional exit for its majority investor, Fluor. Fluor will convert its Class B units into Class A common stock to sell on the market. NuScale says that this will be a "controlled program" to preserve equity value, but I think the stock rightfully plunged on the news. In exchange for this, Fluor agreed to waive certain economic rights, including claims under a tax receivables agreement, which improves NuScale's long-term economic position despite the near-term shift in its shareholder base.

Right now, the stock trades much lower than it did throughout 2025, but the news around this company isn’t great. It looks like investors are leaving, insiders are selling, and even though they are the only company with a design approval, no revenues are expected in the next 4 years.

The stock isn’t cheap by any metric, although it can surge on different speculations of the market. I see no real justification for the levels we’ve seen in 2025, but they may repeat themselves if this bullish AI rally continues. So if you are in for the game, that’s ok, but based on the fundamentals, there is little to catch here.

NANO Nuclear Energy

NANO Nuclear is very different from NuScale. They are trying to bypass the grid and not rely on it whatsoever by developing microreactors (specifically models named ZEUS and ODIN). These are tiny compared to NuScale, producing anywhere from 1 MW to 20 MW. They also have KRONOS, and they present it as the solution for data centers, but, reading a little about this technology, I am very skeptical that it will ever get approved. They want to connect many small reactors together (about 60, if I understood correctly) to power a data center without needing water or a grid. I have read about several new developments that want to do something similar with less dangerous products, like batteries, and this is usually harder than just connecting them and turning them on.

For now, their microreactors are not even close to design approval, not to mention a combined design with 60 parts. Remembering that the review process for NuScale took 41 months, while the technology wasn’t new, it looks like Nano may not get it until 2030, but the demand is coming way before that. Not an ideal situation to be in.

Their moat, though, is in their business model, as it’s the only company that is trying to become a vertically integrated nuclear company. They have subsidiaries dedicated to fuel (HALEU Energy Fuel) and transportation. They are trying to secure the supply chain for the special High-Assay Low-Enriched Uranium that the advanced reactors need and developing the logistics to physically move nuclear material. The idea is that even if their reactor takes a decade to get approved, they might earn something from selling fuel and transport services to other nuclear companies in the meantime. While this is an interesting niche to take, a company that does fuel delivery to nuclear facilities should not be valued as an SMR company.

NANO Nuclear Energy is a company with zero revenue; their financials are purely about "burn" and "runway." At the same time, their strategy has been surprisingly defensive. By the end of 2025, largely thanks to a private placement, they got almost $580 million. For a company of their size and stage, this is an immense amount of capital, which will be enough to keep going for a few years.

Our cash position increased to approximately $580 million following an October 2025 private placement. We view our strong cash position and proven ability to raise funding at scale as a meaningful differentiator. With current cash on hand and our access to the public capital markets, we are well positioned to accelerate the licensing and commercialization of the KRONOS MMR while maintaining the flexibility to expand our vertical integration through disciplined M&A and potential strategic partnerships.

The company is buying assets with that money, so they are not being spent for nothing. They are buying physical facilities and investing in vertical integration, like their uranium enrichment. They are using their inflated stock valuation to buy the supply chain, which can prove to be a very smart move when the reactor is ready and operational.

Ultimately, both companies are asking investors to be okay with a heavy dilution, but they are different in this as well. NuScale is diluting you for a specific commercial destination that is visible on the horizon, and NANO is diluting you to survive until the approval, and nobody knows how many years that will take.

Conclusion

Comparing NuScale Power and NANO Nuclear Energy shows a major difference in how the nuclear companies are attempting to solve the AI energy crisis.

NuScale is using Light Water Reactor technology, meaning they have accepted a lower ceiling of innovation in exchange for a higher floor of regulatory safety, and according to the approval, it worked. For investors, the risk here is execution. I don't know if they can survive the cash burn and manage the construction logistics to get modules online by 2030.

NANO Nuclear thinks that the grid itself is the problem and that the solution is to bypass it entirely with microreactors. However, the risk here is enormous because they are years away from the design approval, not to mention the construction, client attraction, etc. Their vertical integration (fuel and transport) is an interesting idea, but I am not sure it will be able to meaningfully support Nano.

It looks like the speed of AI is much faster than the speed of nuclear, the same as for the regular grid, and other solutions that are available today, besides renewable energy. Hyperscalers need gigawatts of power now, and neither of these companies can provide that.

If you are looking for exposure to the immediate infrastructure, NuScale is the clearer, albeit diluted, path. But if you believe the future of energy is mobile, and you have the stomach to wait for years, NANO looks like a higher speculative upside. In either case, you aren't investing in today's and not even tomorrow’s revenue, and to be honest, I started breaking these two to pick the one that I like more or even call both a buy, but after this analysis, I decided not to pursue this idea.

data.title: Atlassian Q2 Preview: Is The SaaS Apocalypse Over?
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Atlassian Corporation (TEAM), I reiterated my Buy rating with a price target of $214, as the company beat revenue and earnings estimates in their Q1 FY26 and raised full-year guidance while accelerating cRPO (Current Remaining Performance Obligations) and momentum with Rovo.

Unfortunately, I have been terribly wrong with my bullish ratings on Atlassian through all of 2025, as I underestimated the magnitude of multiple contractions that took place with the company’s stock as well as in the entire SaaS complex.

Most recently, with the launch of Anthropic's (ANTHRO) Claude Cowork on January 12, the SaaS complex is going through yet another apocalypse, amid fears that its “seat-based” pricing model will be rendered obsolete by Agentic AI.

While many software companies, including Atlassian, are utilizing a hybrid pricing model that includes both a “seat-based” subscription and a “usage-based” consumption model, investors are not convinced. That is because, unlike the "infrastructure" layer, the software complex is yet to see a meaningful reacceleration in revenue. This applies to Atlassian as well.

The company is scheduled to report its Q2 FY26 earnings on 02/05. However, until investors get clarity on how Agentic AI reshapes traditional SaaS, we may not see a sustained improvement in the company’s forward earnings multiple, which currently sits below the sector median, even when its earnings are expected to grow in the high teens over the next few years with no meaningful downward revision.

While the stock is terribly oversold with a potential short-term rebound of 21%, I will remain on the sidelines and instead consolidate my position in ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW), which I have earlier discussed.

Brief Recap Of Q1 FY26 Earnings

When Atlassian reported its Q1 FY26 earnings at the end of October, revenue grew 21% YoY to $1.46B, driven by Cloud revenue growing by 26% YoY, as well as a 160 basis point benefit from the impact of the Data Center EOL revenue recognition announcement.

Plus, earlier in September, management had also announced plans to EOL (end-of-life) their Data Center products over a multi-year period while introducing their “Atlassian Ascend” program to spearhead their customers’ migration journey from the Data Center to the Cloud to unlock the full potential of their “enterprise-grade” platform.

During the quarter, Atlassian also advanced its “System of Work” product innovation roadmap with “Collections,” where the idea is to move away from standalone apps to a curated set of apps and agents that are built on the Atlassian Platform. In addition to their Teamwork Collection, which includes Jira, Confluence, and Loom, along with their AI solution, Rovo, they also introduced Service and Software Collections, leading to a larger number of multi-year commitments and cRPO accelerating on a sequential basis to 30%.

For the full year, management raised its revenue guidance higher by 280 basis points to 20.8%, driven by Cloud revenue outlook, a greater volume of migrations from the Data Center to Cloud, and an expected benefit of 320 basis points from the Data Center EOL revenue recognition impact. When it comes to profitability, management also raised guidance for gross and operating margins to 86.5% (up 100 basis points) and 25.5% (up 150 basis points), respectively, driven primarily by a 200 basis point tailwind from their Data Center EOL revenue recognition impact.

Putting The State Of Current Software Apocalypse In Context

However, the SaaS complex has been under immense pressure in 2026 so far. This includes companies like Atlassian, ServiceNow, Salesforce, Inc. (CRM), and monday.com Ltd. (MNDY) that are down over 15%+, hugely underperforming the S&P 500 Index (SP500).

While the SaaS complex also struggled in 2025, the latest round of sell-off has been triggered by Anthropic's launch of its Claude Cowork on January 12, 2026. Essentially, Claude Cowork is an autonomous AI agent that can manage files, generate documents from notes, create spreadsheets, and organize workflows without human intervention. This resulted in investor concern that with AI agents handling a wide range of tasks autonomously, companies will now need fewer human workers and therefore fewer software licenses, which threatens the traditional “seat-based” predictable ARR model of software companies.

As Shay Boloor, Chief Market Strategist at Futurum Equities, pointed out below,

“Seat-based pricing is a proxy for labor, so if your revenue model depends on how many humans are doing the work, then you are implicitly betting that digital output scales by adding people. Agentic AI breaks that assumption when buyers believe outcomes can scale without seats, the premium multiple collapses first, long before revenue does.

That’s why nearly every CRM has gone from 40x FCF in 2023 to ~16–18x today. The market isn’t saying these products have no value. It’s saying the old valuation logic can’t be trusted anymore.”

Essentially, what we are seeing, therefore, is a massive repricing in enterprise SaaS companies.

Having said that, there have been a few exceptions in the software complex that have shielded themselves from the massacre.

As I discussed in my previous post on MongoDB: Following Palantir’s Playbook, the reason why both these companies have outperformed at a time when the entire SaaS complex is under a repricing attack is that they positioned themselves as essential “AI infrastructure” plays rather than “AI-disruptible” software applications, while simultaneously benefiting from consumption-based business models.

Does that mean it is time to dump traditional SaaS companies like Atlassian?

Aaron Levie, CEO at Box, put out this thoughtful post on X, where he argued that the proliferation of AI agents will expand the size of software markets massively. This is how he frames it below.

“I’d argue that in a world of 100X more AI agents than people in an enterprise, the value of the systems of record and tools agents will use will go up, not down. Because in this new world, software provides the guardrails on which agents can operate successfully within an enterprise and gives them the underlying tools to use to be more productive themselves and work alongside people.

For instance, if you ask an AI agent to generate 10X the number of outbound email and marketing messages, where will all those agents find the data to work with, and where will the leads end up going to have a human sales rep act on them? Almost certainly some form of a CRM system. Or when an AI Agent processes reviews invoices and transactions, where will they enter this data when they’re done to kick off a supply chain workflow? Again, an existing or reinvented ERP system. As long as we have humans and agents interacting with one another, there will need to be deterministic software that bridges these interactions seamlessly.

And as a result of all of these new agentic users within our software systems, the size of the enterprise software market is going to increase massively.”

At the same time, he also points out that the business model of software companies will have to evolve from “seat-based” pricing to “consumption/usage-based” pricing. To be fair, we are already seeing that happening with several software companies, including Atlassian, where they are integrating Agentic AI and monetizing it through a “consumption-based” pricing model.

For instance, in Atlassian’s case, the company utilizes a hybrid model with a mix of premium subscriptions that give enterprises access to its core AI agents as well as usage-based credits for its Rovo platform.

What is also important to note is that the market may be overestimating the speed of disruption and completely discounting the possibility that existing SaaS players can successfully adapt and compete in Agentic AI.

However, unlike the revenue bump that we have seen in the "infrastructure" layer, traditional SaaS is yet to demonstrate meaningful signs of AI monetization. As Shay Boloor points out,

“Until investors have clarity on who captures pricing power when outcomes scale without seats, money stays tight, multiples stay compressed, and software trades like a sector in exile.”

What To Expect in Q2 FY26?

Atlassian is scheduled to report its Q2 FY26 earnings on 02/05, where revenue and earnings per share are expected to grow at 20.03% and 19.00% YoY during the quarter.

As per consensus estimates, both revenue and earnings per share are expected to accelerate in the following two quarters.

However, as Citi analyst Fatima Boolani pointed out, their Data Center EOL announcement has complicated their financial model mechanics, which in turn is boosting FY26 revenue outlook but creating tougher comps for FY27. However, what is even more concerning, according to the analyst, is the quality and cadence of topline growth, which she expects to be weak outside of pricing increases, migration from Data Center to Cloud, and accounting impacts.

As we can see below, forward revenue growth expectations remain muted, with analysts projecting no lift as such from AI monetization.

At this point, in order for Atlassian stock to regain investor confidence, I believe a simple beat and raise won’t do. Instead, management will have to demonstrate meaningful traction across both its “seat-based” and “usage-based” pricing models, which points to faster-than-expected revenue growth in FY26 and acceleration beyond that.

Assessing The Investment Thesis & Conclusions

With the SaaS complex getting massively repriced as its “seat-based” recurring ARR comes under attack, it is important to note that on a 6M basis, there have been no meaningful downward revisions to Atlassian’s forward revenue and earnings growth estimates.

However, with no signs of revenue reacceleration ahead, investors are convinced that the entire SaaS complex is dead and incapable of adapting and competing.

Yet in all of this, one may argue that a forward revenue growth in the high teens is quite strong, while Atlassian has been innovating on its Agentic AI product roadmap with Rovo to redefine the company’s “System of Work” vision. In its Q1 FY26 earnings, monthly active users of Rovo grew 50% QoQ to 3.5M, with over 2.4M business workflows orchestrated through Rovo as of late 2025.

At the moment, Atlassian is trading at a forward P/E ratio of just 24, which is lower than the sector median. In fact, over the last year, its forward PE ratio has contracted by over 70%, which is terribly disappointing, especially when its earnings per share are projected to grow in the high teens range over the next 2-3 years, as can be seen below.

This forward P/E ratio gets even further compressed to a little over 17 when taking projected FY28 earnings per share into account.

Like I said earlier, until investors get further clarity into how Agentic AI reshapes traditional SaaS, the P/E ratio may remain depressed for longer than one may expect.

However, given that the daily RSI is 19, the stock is terribly oversold at current levels and should stage a rebound to its 10-day moving average (which is trending lower) to $143 per share, which represents an upside of 21% from current levels.

So, while short-term investors can play for a rebound, I will remain on the sidelines with Atlassian until we get clarity and instead consolidate my enterprise SaaS position in my highest conviction name, that is, ServiceNow.

data.title: Microsoft: Something Doesn't Add Up
data.description:
Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) reported a solid fiscal first quarter in 2026, beating both top and bottom line expectations by 3.03% and 12.73%, respectively. But, despite the strong momentum, Microsoft's stock has dropped ~15% since the earnings release. This logic doesn't add up.

I agree, most of the setback has stemmed from fears about AI spending and sustainability, since Microsoft is one of those that are betting heavily on AI infrastructure. Even so, the fundamentals haven’t changed, and there are still several factors that point to continued momentum in 2026.

For those reasons, my Strong Buy stance for Microsoft remains in place—despite the secular headwinds.

Microsoft’s fiscal first-quarter 2026 earnings recap

While Microsoft's second quarter numbers are due out on January 28, 2025, for context, I thought I'd include this little recap of what happened in Q1.

In the most recent quarter, Microsoft’s revenue rose 18% to $77.7 billion, driven by its Intelligent Cloud segment, which contributed $30.9 billion alone to the top line. On that subject, this segment’s revenue increased 28% compared to the same period last year. Within that sphere, Azure grew 40% in constant currency, with AI services (Dynamics 365) delivering ~18 percentage points to that growth. This tells me that customers are indeed pouring money into AI integration as I write this.

In terms of profitability, gross profit rose 18% to $53.6 billion, while gross margin stayed relatively flat at ~69%. That’s impressive considering that the company is building data centers at an unusually high pace. Meanwhile, operating income climbed 24% to $37.9 billion, and net income rose 12% to $27.7 billion, or $3.73 per share. That said, the bottom line figures are a tad lower as Microsoft took a $3.1 billion charge related to its OpenAI (OPENAI) investment.

Looking at the balance sheet, the company ended the quarter with $102 billion in cash and short-term investments against a long-term debt of $35.4 billion. Zooming out, Microsoft’s total assets reached $636.3 billion with total liabilities of ~$270 billion. That’s a pretty healthy financial position.

Speaking of cash, its cash flow paints an even brighter picture. Operating cash flow for the quarter increased 32% to $45 billion, while free cash flow jumped 33% to $25.7 billion. So despite record spending on infrastructure, cash generation is actually accelerating.

For Q2, management is guiding revenue to come in between $79.5 and $80.6 billion, up between 14% and 16% YoY. Also, Intelligent Cloud revenue is expected to come in between $32.25 and $32.55 billion, or ~27% higher year-over-year. Meanwhile, Azure is expected to grow 37% in constant currency. If any of this materializes, it should more than help clear up uncertainties around AI computing demand.

Of course, CapEx is also expected to increase from here, exceeding that of fiscal 2025 growth rates, and more importantly, computing capacity remains limited through at least the end of the fiscal year.

How is MSFT stock performing?

Microsoft stock currently trades at ~$460 per share at the time of this publication. Over the past six months, MSFT stock is down roughly ~9%, while it's up ~8% on a one-year scale. Zoom out five years, and the stock is up ~113%, mainly driven by its cloud infrastructure business.

In terms of valuation, Microsoft trades at a FWD price-to-book ratio of roughly 8x, which is about 60% higher than the sector median. As a long-term shareholder myself, I’d agree that Microsoft’s real value comes from its relationships with practically every single Fortune 500 company and its dominant position in the AI market—so this premium makes sense.

However, the company’s FWD P/E ratio (GAAP) sits at ~29x, which is around 8% lower than the sector median—despite being a notably dominant player in AI and cloud computing. At this point, Microsoft is the cheapest in the Magnificent 7 lineup, considering traditional valuation metrics:
